other_observers: Glenn Walbek; first found by Jason Beason the day before, but identified by him as Trumpeter Swans. Re-identified by Glenn and myself
description: the birds were obvious swans, very large in size, significantly larger than nearby Canada Geese, with very long necks, small heads, and completely white plumage. The neck length, when laid out on the back, was a bit shorter than length of the body. The bills were black, and fairly small (for a swan), with a noticeable concavity to the culmen. The cheek contour was angled about halfway along its legth, and not a smooth line. There was a small yellow spot at the base of the bills on both birds, though this is not visible on the photos. The skin between the bill and eye was black, but very thin, barely connecting the bill to the eye. The head was rounded, with the peak on the middle of the crown.Behaviors: the birds were seen swimming near the edge of the lake, and loafing along the shore.Call: none heardPlumage: adult
